question: How do you decide between local component state, Context, Redux/Zustand, and server state libraries (React Query/SWR) for state management?
reference_answer: Match the tool to the kind of state. (1) Local component state (useState) — anything used only by one component or its immediate children. Default. Most state should live here. (2) Lifted state — when 2-3 sibling components need the same value, lift it to the nearest common ancestor. Still no library. (3) Context — when state is global-but-stable (theme, current user, locale) and changes infrequently. Avoid for high-frequency state — every Context update re-renders all consumers. (4) Global client state library (Redux, Zustand, Jotai) — when you have complex client-side state that crosses many components, requires actions / selectors / dev tools, and high update frequency. Smaller apps almost never need this; Zustand is much lighter than Redux for the same job. (5) Server state library (React Query, SWR) — for any data that comes from an API. They handle caching, deduplication, background refetching, stale-while-revalidate, optimistic updates, and pagination — features you would otherwise implement badly in Redux. Common anti-pattern: putting fetched data into Redux/Context — manually rebuilds what React Query gives you for free. Decision tree: is this server data? → React Query. Is it global app config? → Context. Is it complex local-with-actions? → Zustand. Otherwise → useState. Most apps end up: useState heavy, React Query for API, Context for theme/auth, no Redux at all.

content: Local state (useState) by default; lift only when needed
Context for stable global state (theme, user)
Redux/Zustand for complex client state with frequent updates
React Query/SWR for server state — distinct category
clarity: Calls out 'don't put server data in Redux' anti-pattern

question: You're choosing the rendering strategy for an e-commerce site with product pages, a personalised homepage, and a marketing blog. Walk through your choices.
reference_answer: Different routes have different needs; modern frameworks (Next.js, Remix, Nuxt) let you choose per route. (1) Marketing blog — content changes occasionally, no personalisation, SEO critical. SSG with on-demand revalidation — pages are pre-built at deploy time, regenerated when content changes. Fastest possible first paint, free CDN caching, near-zero hosting cost. (2) Product pages — content updates frequently (price, stock), some personalisation possible (recommendations), SEO critical for organic traffic. ISR or SSR with caching — pre-render at build, revalidate every N minutes or on stock changes; OR SSR with edge caching and short TTL (60s). Fast and fresh. Personalised slots (recommendations) hydrate client-side without blocking initial paint. (3) Personalised homepage — different per user (logged-in dashboard or recommendations), no SEO benefit, behind login. SSR for first paint with personalised data, then CSR for interactions; OR SPA-style if it's behind a login wall and SEO doesn't matter. Cross-cutting concerns: shared design system; image optimisation (next/image); data fetching layer (React Query) for client interactions; analytics that respects consent. The key principle: pick rendering per route based on (does this need SEO? how often does content change? is it personalised?). Mistake: forcing one strategy everywhere — SSR for marketing wastes server resources, SSG for personalised user data is impossible.

content: Picks rendering per route based on freshness, SEO, personalisation
Specific choices: SSG for blog, ISR/SSR for products, SSR/CSR for personal
Names how personalisation can hydrate over SSG
Cross-cutting concerns mentioned
clarity: Frames it as 'different routes, different strategies'

question: Walk through how you would build an accessible custom dropdown component (like an autocomplete) from scratch.
reference_answer: Honestly: do not unless you have to. The accessibility surface area for custom comboboxes is enormous — use a library (Headless UI, Reach UI, Radix UI, downshift) that has solved it. If you must build it, follow the WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices Guide combobox pattern. Structure: an input with role='combobox', aria-expanded, aria-controls pointing to the listbox; a listbox (role='listbox') with options (role='option'); aria-activedescendant on the input pointing to the focused option without moving DOM focus. Keyboard: ArrowDown/Up cycles through options; Home/End jump to first/last; Enter selects; Esc closes; typing filters and announces option count via aria-live; Tab closes the dropdown and moves focus normally. Screen reader testing: VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS — they each behave slightly differently with combobox, requiring testing across all three. Visual: visible focus ring on the input AND a distinct visual indicator on the focused option (background colour); 4.5:1 contrast ratio for text; respect prefers-reduced-motion for any open/close animations. Mobile: mobile screen readers (TalkBack, VoiceOver iOS) treat custom comboboxes very differently; sometimes a native <select> is the right call on mobile and your fancy combobox only on desktop. Common failures: trapping focus inside the dropdown with no escape; dropdown closes on click outside but ignores keyboard; option focus moves DOM focus and disorients screen readers; no announcement when results change. The maxim: 'first rule of ARIA is do not use ARIA' — use a native select first if you can; only build a custom combobox when the requirement genuinely needs it.

content: Recommends using a tested library first; build from scratch only with cause
Names ARIA combobox pattern (combobox, listbox, options, aria-activedescendant)
Keyboard interactions specified (arrow keys, Home/End, Enter, Esc)
Tested with multiple screen readers
clarity: Calls out mobile / native-select fallback
structure: Use library, then if must, structure, keyboard, testing, common failures

question: What are XSS, CSRF, and CSP? How do they relate, and what's the modern defence?
reference_answer: XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) — attacker injects malicious JS into your page; the JS runs with full access to user's session, cookies, and DOM. Source: any user-controllable input rendered without escaping. Modern defence: (1) Frameworks that escape by default (React, Vue render text, not HTML, unless you opt in via dangerouslySetInnerHTML / v-html); (2) Server-side input validation; (3) Output encoding for any HTML you must build manually; (4) CSP (below) as defence in depth. CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) — attacker tricks a user's browser into making a state-changing request to your site, riding on the user's session cookie. Modern defence: SameSite=Lax or Strict cookies (default in modern browsers, blocks most CSRF automatically); CSRF tokens for sensitive endpoints; double-submit cookies; require explicit auth headers (Bearer tokens) which browsers do not send automatically. CSP (Content Security Policy) — HTTP header that tells the browser which sources of scripts, styles, images, etc. are allowed; unauthorised resources are blocked, drastically reducing impact of XSS that slipped through. A strong CSP: script-src 'self' 'nonce-XYZ'; object-src 'none'; base-uri 'none'; with nonces on every inline script and a violations report endpoint. They relate: XSS is the largest threat; CSRF is much smaller now thanks to SameSite cookies; CSP is the safety net for both. Modern stack: React/Vue (escape by default) + SameSite=Lax cookies + strong CSP with nonces + auth via tokens in Authorization header is a strong baseline. Anti-patterns: 'we sanitise on the client' (run server-side too); 'CSP unsafe-inline' (defeats most of CSP).

content: XSS = injection of attacker JS; defence is escaping + CSP
CSRF = forged state-change with cookie; defence is SameSite + tokens
CSP = HTTP header restricting allowed sources
Modern defaults that solve most of these (frameworks, SameSite)
clarity: Identifies XSS as still the biggest risk
structure: Three threats, each defence, modern stack

question: Explain advanced TypeScript features like generics, conditional types, and mapped types. When are they worth the cost?
reference_answer: Generics — type variables that let a function or type work with multiple types while preserving type safety. function identity<T>(value: T): T returns the same type it was called with. Used everywhere: array methods, Promises, React component props. Worth it: yes, almost always; the cost is negligible and the type-safety gain is large. Conditional types — type X = T extends U ? A : B selects between two types based on whether T is assignable to U. Combined with infer to extract sub-types: ReturnType<T> = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never. Used heavily in library code and utility types. Mapped types — type X<T> = { [K in keyof T]: ... } iterates over keys and transforms them. Partial<T>, Required<T>, Pick<T,K>, Omit<T,K> are all mapped types. Used to derive types from existing ones rather than duplicating. When worth the cost: in shared library code and utility types — write once, paid back many times. In application code, usually not — clear, simple types are easier to read and refactor than clever ones. The trap: type-puzzle complexity where the type definition is harder to understand than the runtime code it describes. If a colleague needs 5 minutes to read your type, it has overshot. Rule of thumb: prefer 'is this readable?' over 'is this maximally precise?'. TypeScript's value is communication and tooling, not Olympic-grade type proofs.

content: Generics: type variables for reusable functions/types — used widely
Conditional types: extends ? A : B; powers utility types
Mapped types: iterate over keys to transform; Partial, Pick, etc.
Worth it for libraries, often overkill in app code
clarity: Calls out 'is it readable?' over 'is it precise?'
structure: Three features, when worth the cost

question: Your production bundle is 1.2 MB gzipped and the team wants it under 400 KB. How do you investigate and reduce it?
reference_answer: Investigate first. Run a bundle analyser (webpack-bundle-analyzer, source-map-explorer, vite-bundle-visualizer) to see what is in the bundle: which dependencies are largest, which are duplicated, what app code dominates. The wins almost always come from a few large items, not many small ones. Common big wins: (1) Replace heavy deps with lighter alternatives — moment.js (300 KB) → date-fns or dayjs (5-15 KB); lodash full → lodash-es with tree-shaking, or just a few cherry-picked imports; chart.js → Chart.js's modular tree-shake-friendly version; full Material UI → use the components actually needed. (2) Tree-shake — import { specific } from 'lib' instead of import lib from 'lib'; ensure sideEffects is set in package.json so the bundler can drop unused code. (3) Code-split routes and heavy features — dynamic import() so users only pay for what they hit; Webpack/Vite makes this seamless. (4) Audit polyfills — modern browsers do not need most of them; use browserslist to target current browsers and skip ancient ones. (5) Remove dead code that no one is using anymore. (6) Compress images (often co-bundled): WebP/AVIF, srcset, lazy load. (7) Server compression: ensure Brotli is enabled (smaller than gzip). Track bundle size in CI — fail the build if it grows past a budget. The 400 KB target is reachable for most apps with disciplined dependency choice and route splitting; without tracking, bundle size silently bloats with every PR.

content: Investigate with bundle analyser before changing
Replace heavy deps with lighter alternatives (concrete examples)
Tree-shaking + code splitting
Bundle size budget in CI as long-term discipline
clarity: Wins concentrated in few big items, not many small
